name: Relief
short_description: A scalable actuated tabletop shape display with 120 motorized pins that renders and animates 3D forms while sensing users pushing and pulling on its surface.
long_description: Relief is an actuated tabletop display whose malleable surface is driven by an array of 120 individually addressable motorized pins, built on low-cost open-source hardware and software to make actuated shape displays scalable. Beyond physically rendering 3D models such as geographic terrain, each pin senses user input like pushing and pulling, turning the display into a bidirectional tangible interface for both output and input. As an early MIT Media Lab shape display, Relief is a direct ancestor of inFORM and Recompose, and it matters to physical AI and interaction design as a foundational demonstration of shape-changing interfaces in which the physical material itself becomes the medium of interaction.
ai_note: Relief is a motorized pin display with mechanical push/pull sensing and no machine learning; intelligence resides in the user and the rendered models, not in any AI component.

images.caption: The Relief actuated tabletop shape display rendering a 3D terrain surface with its array of 120 motorized pins.
images.alt_text: A tabletop grid of many white cylindrical pins raised to different heights to form a rolling three-dimensional terrain surface, lit by overhead projection.
